title: Aion Dashboard — Monitoring Web UI for the Standalone Server
intention: Build the human-facing operational web UI for aion-server: a browser application that lets an operator see which workflows are running and which have failed, drill into a single execution's full event history rendered as a timeline, and watch events stream live as they happen. This cluster builds the frontend only. It consumes the HTTP/gRPC list/query/history API and the WebSocket event stream that the server (cluster AW) exposes and hosts as static assets; it does not run the engine, persist anything, or define the API. It must match the existing house frontend stack (React 19 + TypeScript + Vite + Tailwind v4 + Biome + Bun, shadcn/Radix, TanStack Query, a singleton WebSocket manager with reconnect, generated types from Rust) exactly, so it reads as part of the same codebase as apps/web. When done, an operator pointed at a running aion-server can observe every workflow in every namespace, drill into any execution's full history, and watch events stream in real time — with production-grade loading, empty, error, and reconnection behaviour throughout.
problem: A workflow engine without observability is a black box; operators trust what they can see. Aion's server exposes the right primitives — a query/list API, a per-workflow history API, and a first-class WebSocket event stream — but primitives are not a product. Someone must render them.

The list must scale: a production server holds thousands of workflows across namespaces, so the list view must paginate against the query API and filter by type, status, and time range without loading every history.

History is the hard part: a workflow's value to an operator is its ordered event history (activities, timers, signals, child workflows, terminal outcome). A flat JSON dump is useless; a timeline keyed off the event envelope (sequence, timestamp, type) tells the story at a glance.

Live is the differentiator: Aion streams events as they happen, so the detail view should grow as events arrive and the list should reflect status transitions live — requiring a real WebSocket subscription model (per-workflow on detail, filtered/firehose on list) with reconnection and resync discipline for long-lived operator sessions.

Production UIs fail gracefully: every async surface needs loading, empty, and error states; a dropped socket must reconnect and resync rather than silently freeze; namespace selection (the server's multi-tenancy) must scope every query and subscription.

It must match the existing house frontend stack (apps/web) rather than inventing a new one, reusing the same tools, feature-folder conventions, React Query usage, WebSocket manager pattern, and generated-types pipeline.

solution.summary: A standalone Vite SPA, apps/aion-dashboard, built on the same stack as apps/web, that aion-server serves as static assets (AW owns hosting; this cluster owns the bundle). It talks to the server over REST for list/query/history and WebSocket for the live event stream. Organised as house feature folders: api/ (a typed REST client and an Aion-specialised singleton WebSocket manager with reconnect/resync); features/workflow-list (paginated, filterable list driven by React Query against the query API, with a filtered WS subscription updating rows live); features/workflow-detail (the history viewer — loads a workflow's full event history and renders it as a sequence-ordered timeline with activity/timer/signal/child/lifecycle entries correlated by id, with a per-workflow WS subscription appending live events); features/live-feed (shared subscription lifecycle, reconnect/resync, connection indicator, firehose); features/namespace (namespace selection scoping every query and subscription); components/ (shadcn/Radix primitives plus status badge, event icon, timeline row, empty/error/loading panels); app/ (router, providers, layout). Wire types (Event, WorkflowSummary, WorkflowFilter, WorkflowStatus, namespace) come from aion-core/aion-proto via the house generated-types pipeline — the dashboard never hand-defines a wire shape the engine owns. The boundary is load-bearing: the dashboard consumes the AW contract; it defines no endpoint, no WS protocol, and no hosting mechanism, isolating any not-yet-pinned contract detail in the api module.

decisions.title: Frontend-only; consume the AW contract
decisions.choice: This cluster builds only the browser UI. The REST API (endpoints, request/response shapes, pagination, namespace param), the WebSocket endpoint (URL, subscribe/unsubscribe protocol, event frame shape, firehose/filter semantics), and static-asset hosting are all cluster AW. The dashboard codes against that contract as fixed and treats AW (which forwards the engine's EventFilter subscription model from AE/AT) as the protocol owner.

decisions.title: Standalone Vite SPA matching the apps/web stack
decisions.choice: The dashboard is apps/aion-dashboard, a Vite SPA on the exact apps/web stack: React 19, TypeScript, Tailwind v4, Bun, Biome (100-char), shadcn/Radix primitives, TanStack Query. No new framework, styling system, or state library. Rejected: building it inside apps/web (it ships with the aion-server binary, a different deployment unit) and inventing a fresh stack (it must read as house code).

decisions.title: Wire types from the generated-types pipeline
decisions.choice: Event, WorkflowSummary, WorkflowStatus, WorkflowFilter, and namespace types come from aion-core/aion-proto via Rust #[derive(TS)] → src/types/generated/, never hand-edited — the same discipline apps/web follows. The dashboard imports these; it does not redefine a type the engine owns. Local-only view models (timeline entry, filter form state) are hand-written in feature types.ts.

decisions.title: The event history is a sequence-ordered timeline
decisions.choice: The detail view renders the aion-core Event enum as a vertical timeline, one entry per event, ordered strictly by the envelope sequence number (the same ordering the engine and store guarantee). Each entry shows envelope (sequence, timestamp), a kind icon/colour, and a one-line payload summary expandable on demand. Rejected: a flat JSON/table dump — it does not tell the execution's story.

decisions.title: Correlate multi-event lifecycles into groups
decisions.choice: ActivityScheduled→Started→Completed/Failed are correlated by ActivityId into one expandable group (retries show as repeated Failed attempts); Timer events correlate by TimerId; child-workflow events correlate by child id and link to the child's detail view. An operator sees an activity's full lifecycle as one unit, not four disconnected rows.

decisions.title: Singleton WebSocket manager with subscribe/reconnect/resync
decisions.choice: The live stream uses a singleton WebSocket manager modelled on apps/web's (bounded reconnect, handler set, connect/disconnect notifications), specialised for the Aion protocol: subscribe by filter (per-workflow on detail, filtered on list, firehose on feed), receive Event frames. On drop it reconnects with backoff and the active view resyncs — detail re-fetches history or replays after last-seen sequence (the after_seq pattern apps/web's assistant socket already uses), list re-runs its query. A connection indicator is always present.

decisions.title: De-duplicate history-fetch vs live-stream overlap
decisions.choice: Around the subscription boundary the same event can arrive both in the initial history fetch and the live stream. The timeline keys events by sequence number and de-duplicates, so an event is never rendered twice and ordering stays correct as live events slot in.

decisions.title: Server state through TanStack Query; live updates patch caches
decisions.choice: All REST reads (list/query, history, namespaces) go through TanStack Query. Live WebSocket events update the relevant query cache or local view state rather than triggering a fresh fetch per event. Rejected: bespoke fetch-into-useState for server data — it loses caching, invalidation, and the loading/error machinery Query provides.

decisions.title: Explicit four-state rendering on every async surface
decisions.choice: Loading (layout-reserving skeletons, not bare spinners), empty (distinct 'no workflows match' / 'no events yet'), error (cause + retry affordance, never a blank panel), and connected-vs-disconnected (persistent connection indicator). No surface spins indefinitely or blanks on failure.

decisions.title: Namespace selection scopes everything
decisions.choice: A namespace context holds the selected namespace (the server's multi-tenancy). Every query and every subscription is scoped by it; switching namespace re-scopes both the active query and the live subscription. No view issues an unscoped query when a namespace is selected.

decisions.title: Read-only observability; no operator actions in scope
decisions.choice: This cluster is observability — list, history, live feed. It is read-only over the monitoring surface. Operator actions (terminate/cancel a workflow from the UI) are deliberately out of scope; if wanted later they layer on AL's command API. This keeps the cluster focused and off the critical path.

constraints.description: Match the apps/web stack exactly: React 19, TypeScript, Vite, Tailwind v4, Bun, Biome (100-char), shadcn/Radix primitives, TanStack Query. No new framework, styling system, or state library.

Wire types come from the house generated-types pipeline (Rust #[derive(TS)] → src/types/generated/). The dashboard never hand-defines a type aion-core/aion-proto owns and never hand-edits a generated file.

The dashboard consumes the AW API/WebSocket contract; it defines no server endpoint, no WebSocket protocol, and no asset-hosting mechanism. Not-yet-pinned contract details are isolated in the api module for a one-file change.
Feature-folder convention from apps/web: each feature is components/ + hooks/ (+ context/lib as needed) + types.ts + index.ts. Barrels re-export; logic lives in named files.
Server state goes through TanStack Query; live updates flow through the WebSocket manager and patch query caches or local view state. No bespoke fetch-into-useState for server data.
Every async surface renders explicit loading, empty, and error states. No surface spins indefinitely or blanks on failure.

The WebSocket manager reconnects with bounded backoff on drop and the active view resyncs (re-fetch history/query, or replay after last-seen sequence where supported). A connection indicator is always present; a dropped socket never leaves a silently stale view.
The event timeline is ordered strictly by event sequence number and de-duplicates events arriving via both history fetch and live stream.
Biome lint + format clean (bun run check) and tsc --noEmit clean. No lint-disable comments to silence findings.
Namespace selection scopes every query and every subscription; switching namespace re-scopes both. No unscoped query when a namespace is selected.

goals: A standalone apps/aion-dashboard Vite SPA on the apps/web stack, a workspace member with house scripts (dev/build/check/typecheck), buildable to a static bundle servable under a configurable base path by AW.
A typed REST api client over the server's list/query, history, and namespaces endpoints, with all not-yet-pinned contract details isolated in one module.

An Aion-specialised singleton WebSocket manager: subscribe by filter (per-workflow / filtered / firehose), receive Event frames, reconnect with backoff, resync the active view on drop, expose connection status.
A paginated, filterable workflow list view (by type, status, time range, namespace) backed by React Query against the query API, with full loading/empty/error states.
Live row updates on the list from a filtered WebSocket subscription (status transitions, newly-matching workflows).
A workflow detail view loading a full event history from the history API and rendering it as a sequence-ordered timeline.
Timeline entries per Event variant with activity/timer/child lifecycles correlated by id into expandable groups and payload summaries expandable on demand.
A per-workflow WebSocket subscription appending live events to the timeline in sequence order, de-duplicated against loaded history, reflecting the terminal outcome.
Namespace selection scoping every query and subscription, re-scoping both on switch.
A persistent connection indicator and a firehose feed option for unfiltered observability.
Explicit loading/empty/error states and reconnection behaviour across all views; Biome and tsc clean.
non_goals: No server: the REST API, WebSocket endpoint, and static-asset hosting are cluster AW.
No engine: lifecycle, replay, timers, signals, queries are AE/AD/AT.
No client SDK: programmatic start/signal/query/cancel callers are AL; this is the human UI.
No operator actions (terminate/cancel from the UI) — observability only; actions would layer on AL's command API later.
No authentication system — auth/session is AW's concern; the dashboard carries the credential the server expects but defines no auth flow.
No bespoke charting/metrics dashboards — workflow observability, not a Grafana-style metrics surface.
No dedicated mobile layout — desktop-first operator console.

structure.apps/aion-dashboard/vite.config.ts: [AU-001] Vite + React + Tailwind v4, base-path configurable for AW hosting
structure.apps/aion-dashboard/src/types/generated/: [AU-002] TS types generated from aion-core/aion-proto (never hand-edited)
structure.apps/aion-dashboard/src/types/index.ts: [AU-002] local view types + re-export of generated wire types
structure.apps/aion-dashboard/src/lib/api/client.t: [AU-002] typed REST client: listWorkflows/queryWorkflows, getHistory, listNamespaces
structure.apps/aion-dashboard/src/features/workflo: [AU-004] React Query against the query API
